seeds.title: I felt like the eating disorder was taken out of my brain
seeds.body: The constant preoccupation with food and fear of binging just disappeared. I used to think about food all day — what I'd eat, when, how much. On the medication, that voice went quiet. For the first time in years, I could just... live. The nausea when I increased the dose was rough, but nothing compared to the freedom from that mental noise.

seeds.title: 92 pounds in 23 months — and my prediabetes is gone
seeds.body: I'm 64 and I've tried everything. Diets, plans, counting calories for decades. With Ozempic I lost 92 pounds over 23 months. But more than the weight — my prediabetes resolved, my blood pressure normalized, and the polymyalgia rheumatica got better. I still had to put in the effort. The medication doesn't do it for you. But it gave me a fighting chance for the first time.

seeds.title: I don't get fixated on food anymore — and that changed everything
seeds.body: I used to come home after a stressful day at work and immediately binge on junk food. Now I might get a small snack, feel full, and that's it. I'm not thinking about eating more. It's rewired my relationship with food — I eat to stay alive rather than to fill a massive void. I lost 26 kilos in 9 months. But the real transformation was in my head.

seeds.title: After 3 minutes on the treadmill my knees gave out — now I run with my son
seeds.body: My joints were so bad that after three minutes on the treadmill the pain was overwhelming. I was embarrassed, defeated. I couldn't keep up with my son anymore. Nine months and 26 kilos later, I'm at the park with him, running around. He told his friends 'my mum plays with me now.' That sentence is worth more than any number on the scale.

seeds.title: Someone finally turned off the radio in my head — 30 years of food noise gone
seeds.body: Before, food was always on my mind. What would I eat next. How much. Whether I deserved it. On the medication, I might forget to eat. Not because I'm sick — because food just doesn't call me anymore. The research calls it quieting food noise. For me it felt like someone finally turned off a radio that had been playing in my head for 30 years.

seeds.title: The depression hit hard — my third attempt is finally working
seeds.body: Second attempt I lost 20 pounds but the depression at 1mg was really bad. Heartburn, nausea, couldn't exercise. I almost gave up completely. Third attempt with slower titration and a support group — it's finally clicking. What I wish someone told me: this isn't a straight line. Some people need 2 or 3 attempts to find what works for their body.

seeds.title: Stress eating was my coping mechanism for 20 years — it's just gone now
seeds.body: Every hard day ended with food. Argument with my husband? Pizza. Rough day at work? Chips until I felt sick. I didn't even want the food, I wanted the comfort. GLP-1 didn't just reduce my appetite — it removed the compulsion. I had to find new coping mechanisms. That was actually the hard part. But the person I am now? She handles stress with a walk instead of a binge.

seeds.title: Psychiatric hospital visits dropped 42% on semaglutide — the brain connection is real
seeds.body: 2026 research: while taking semaglutide, psychiatric-related hospital visits dropped 42%. Depression risk 44% lower. Anxiety disorders reduced 38%. GLP-1 receptors in the brain's reward system explain why people feel less controlled by food, alcohol, and compulsive behaviors. For millions of people, this isn't just a weight loss drug. It's a mental health breakthrough.
